content.title: Example 1 — Logistics (Python)
content.body: The simplest version: `RoadLogistics` and `SeaLogistics` each override `createTransport()`. The `deliverOrder()` method in the base class never changes.

content.title: Logistics — Complete Example
Example 2 — Cross-Platform Dialog (Java)
content.body: `Dialog.render()` is written once and never changes. Adding `LinuxDialog` requires only creating `LinuxButton` and `LinuxDialog` — nothing else is touched.

content.title: Cross-Platform Dialog — Java

flashcards.q: In the cross-platform dialog example, how can a subclass change the behavior of `render()` without overriding `render()` itself?
flashcards.a: By overriding `createButton()`. The `render()` method calls `createButton()` internally — by changing what `createButton()` returns, the subclass changes the ingredient that `render()` works with.

problems.q: **Implement Factory Method:** Extend the Logistics example to add an `Airplane` transport and `AirLogistics` creator. Write only the new code needed — do not modify existing classes.
problems.hint: You need one Concrete Product and one Concrete Creator. Nothing else changes.

problems.q: **Design from Scratch:** You're building a notification system that sends messages via Email, SMS, and Push. Design the Factory Method structure. List all 4 participants with their names.
problems.a:
- **Product:** `Notification` interface with `send(message)`
- **Concrete Products:** `EmailNotification`, `SMSNotification`, `PushNotification`
- **Creator:** `Notifier` with `create_notification()` factory method and `notify(message)` business logic
- **Concrete Creators:** `EmailNotifier`, `SMSNotifier`, `PushNotifier`
problems.hint: The notifier's notify() method should call create_notification() then send().
